questions.stem: Which layer of Snowflake's architecture is responsible for authentication, query optimization, and metadata management?
questions.explanation: C is correct because the Cloud Services layer handles authentication, query optimization, metadata management, and security — it is the 'brain' of Snowflake. A is wrong because the storage layer is responsible for persisting data in columnar micro-partitions on cloud object storage. B is wrong because the compute layer (virtual warehouses) executes queries using MPP clusters. D is wrong because virtual warehouses are part of the compute layer, not a separate architectural layer.

questions.stem: What is the compressed size range of a Snowflake micro-partition?
questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake micro-partitions range from 50 MB to 500 MB of compressed data. A is wrong because 1–10 MB is too small. C is wrong because 1–5 GB would be too large for a micro-partition. D is wrong because 10–100 MB does not match the documented range.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator runs the same SELECT query twice within 10 minutes, with no data changes to the underlying table between executions. Which statement BEST describes what happens during the second execution?

options.text: The query re-executes on the virtual warehouse but reads from SSD cache instead of object storage.
The query result is returned from the query result cache in the Cloud Services layer and consumes no compute credits.
The query re-executes and consumes compute credits but benefits from metadata pruning.
The virtual warehouse automatically scales up to return the cached result faster.

questions.explanation: B is correct because the query result cache is held in the Cloud Services layer for 24 hours. If the same query is run by the same role, against unchanged data, the cached result is returned without engaging the virtual warehouse, consuming zero compute credits. A is wrong because the SSD (local disk) cache requires the virtual warehouse to be active and would still consume credits. C is wrong because the query does not re-execute when the result cache is hit. D is wrong because scaling the warehouse is not triggered by cached results.

questions.stem: A data engineer resizes a virtual warehouse from X-Small to Small. How many credits per hour will the Small warehouse consume?
questions.explanation: B is correct because each warehouse size doubles the credit consumption. X-Small consumes 1 credit/hr, Small consumes 2 credits/hr, Medium consumes 4 credits/hr, and so on. A is wrong because 1 credit/hr is the X-Small rate. C is wrong because 4 credits/hr is the Medium rate. D is wrong because 8 credits/hr is the Large rate.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Virtual Warehouse Credit Consumption

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature allows a single virtual warehouse to automatically add server clusters to handle concurrent user load spikes, then remove them when demand drops?
questions.explanation: B is correct because multi-cluster warehouses allow Snowflake to automatically provision additional clusters (up to the configured maximum) when query queuing is detected, and remove clusters when demand decreases. A is wrong because auto-suspend/resume controls whether an idle warehouse shuts down, not whether additional clusters are added. C is wrong because the query result cache reduces load but does not add compute capacity. D is wrong because Search Optimization Service improves selective query performance through a pre-built data structure, not dynamic scaling.

questions.stem: A company processes sensitive healthcare data and requires HIPAA compliance. They also need Time Travel retention of up to 90 days. What is the MINIMUM Snowflake edition that satisfies BOTH requirements?
questions.explanation: C is correct because Business Critical is the minimum edition that provides HIPAA compliance support (through a Business Associate Agreement) AND supports up to 90 days of Time Travel. A is wrong because Standard supports only up to 1 day of Time Travel and does not offer HIPAA compliance. B is wrong because Enterprise supports up to 90 days of Time Travel but does NOT provide HIPAA/BAA support. D is wrong because Virtual Private Snowflake does satisfy both requirements, but it is not the MINIMUM — Business Critical already satisfies them at a lower tier.

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following metadata values does Snowflake store for each column within a micro-partition? (Select TWO)
questions.explanation: A and C are correct because Snowflake stores min/max values and null counts per column within each micro-partition. This metadata is used by the pruning engine to skip micro-partitions that cannot contain matching rows. B is wrong because row-level checksums are not stored as per-column metadata in micro-partitions. D is wrong because encryption keys are managed at the account/key hierarchy level, not stored inside micro-partition column metadata.

questions.stem: A multi-cluster warehouse is configured with MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT = 1 and MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 4 using the Standard scaling policy. Under the Standard policy, when does Snowflake add an additional cluster?

options.text: When at least one query is queued for 2 minutes or more.
When a queued query is detected, Snowflake immediately provisions an additional cluster.
When CPU utilization on the active cluster exceeds 80% for 5 consecutive minutes.
When the number of active queries exceeds a user-defined threshold.

questions.explanation: B is correct because under the Standard scaling policy, Snowflake adds a cluster immediately when a query is detected in the queue — the goal is to minimize queuing latency. A is wrong because the 2-minute wait applies to the Economy scaling policy, which is more conservative. C is wrong because CPU utilization is not the trigger for multi-cluster scaling in either policy. D is wrong because there is no user-defined query threshold for multi-cluster scaling.
questions.reference: Snowflake Architecture — Multi-Cluster Warehouse Scaling Policy

questions.stem: Which Snowflake system-defined role is responsible for managing users and roles across the account?
questions.explanation: B is correct because SECURITYADMIN has the privilege to manage all users and roles, including creating and granting roles. A is wrong because SYSADMIN is responsible for creating and managing database objects (warehouses, databases, schemas, tables). C is wrong because USERADMIN can create users and roles but does not have the full account-level privilege to manage all roles as SECURITYADMIN does. D is wrong because PUBLIC is the default role automatically granted to all users and has minimal privileges.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to prevent users from connecting to Snowflake from IP addresses outside of the corporate network. Which object should be used?
questions.explanation: C is correct because a network policy allows administrators to define allowed and blocked IP address ranges, restricting Snowflake access to specific IP addresses. It can be applied at the account or user level. A is wrong because resource monitors track and limit credit consumption, not network access. B is wrong because row access policies control which rows a user can see in a table, not network connectivity. D is wrong because masking policies control how column data is displayed, not network access.

questions.stem: A company wants to hide the Social Security Number column from all users except those with the 'PII_ADMIN' role. Which Snowflake feature should they use?
questions.explanation: B is correct because Dynamic Data Masking allows a masking policy to be applied to a column that conditionally shows full or masked data based on the current role. A is wrong because row access policies control which rows are returned, not how column values are displayed. C is wrong because secure views hide the view definition but do not dynamically mask individual column values based on role. D is wrong because column-level encryption is not a native Snowflake object type — Snowflake uses masking policies for this use case.

questions.stem: A developer runs the following SQL: `GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA mydb.myschema TO ROLE analyst;`. A new table is created in mydb.myschema the next day. Which statement is TRUE about the analyst role's access to the new table?

options.text: The analyst role automatically has SELECT on the new table because the grant was on ALL TABLES.
The analyst role does NOT have SELECT on the new table; the grant applied only to tables that existed at grant time.
The analyst role inherits SELECT on the new table if it was granted the USAGE privilege on the schema.
The analyst role has SELECT on the new table only if the table was created by SYSADMIN.

questions.explanation: B is correct because `GRANT ... ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA` is a one-time grant that applies only to tables existing at the time the grant is executed. Future tables require a separate `GRANT` or the use of `GRANT ... ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA` to automatically include new tables. A is wrong because the grant is not retroactively applied to future objects. C is wrong because USAGE on a schema allows the schema to be accessed but does not grant SELECT on individual tables. D is wrong because the table creator's role does not affect whether analyst inherits privileges.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security — Privilege Management, Future Grants

questions.stem: Which TWO statements correctly describe Snowflake's role hierarchy and privilege inheritance? (Select TWO)

options.text: Privileges granted to a child role are inherited by any parent role that the child role is granted to.
ACCOUNTADMIN is the only role that can grant privileges on account-level objects.
A role can be granted to another role using GRANT ROLE child_role TO ROLE parent_role.
Users can only belong to one role at a time; they must switch roles to use different privileges.

questions.explanation: A and C are correct because privilege inheritance flows upward through the role hierarchy — a parent role inherits all privileges held by child roles granted to it. The syntax `GRANT ROLE child TO ROLE parent` is the correct way to establish this hierarchy. B is wrong because SECURITYADMIN and SYSADMIN can also manage various account-level privileges within their scope. D is wrong because users can have multiple roles granted to them, though they can only have one 'active' role per session; however, the hierarchy means parent roles accumulate privileges from child roles regardless.
questions.reference: Snowflake Security — Role Hierarchy and Privilege Inheritance

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to enforce Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for all users in the account. Which MFA provider does Snowflake natively integrate with?
questions.explanation: C is correct because Snowflake's built-in MFA integration uses Duo Security for push notifications and passcodes. A is wrong because Google Authenticator is not the native MFA provider used by Snowflake's built-in MFA feature. B is wrong because Microsoft Authenticator is not the native MFA provider. D is wrong because Okta Verify is an SSO/SAML provider integration, not the native Snowflake MFA solution.

questions.stem: A data engineer uses the Query Profile in Snowsight to investigate a slow query. Which view does the Query Profile display?

options.text: A list of all tables scanned in alphabetical order
An operator tree showing execution nodes with statistics like rows in/out and partition pruning
A timeline of SQL statements executed in the session
The execution plan in text format identical to EXPLAIN output

questions.explanation: B is correct because the Query Profile displays an interactive operator tree (DAG) where each node shows statistics such as rows processed, bytes processed, and how many partitions were scanned vs. pruned. This helps identify bottlenecks. A is wrong because the Query Profile does not display a simple alphabetical table list. C is wrong because a session history list is available elsewhere in Snowsight, not in the Query Profile. D is wrong because the EXPLAIN command outputs a text-based plan, while the Query Profile is an interactive visual tool with live execution statistics.

questions.stem: A table has been loaded with rows in random order for its `ORDER_DATE` column. Analysts frequently filter on `ORDER_DATE`. After running `ALTER TABLE orders CLUSTER BY (order_date)`, what happens to the existing data?

options.text: The table is immediately and fully reorganized in the background with no credit cost.
Snowflake will periodically recluster the table using compute credits, reorganizing micro-partitions to improve ORDER_DATE locality.
The clustering key only affects newly inserted rows; existing micro-partitions are never reorganized.
The CLUSTER BY statement locks the table until all existing data is reorganized.

questions.explanation: B is correct because when you define a clustering key, Snowflake's automatic reclustering service periodically reorganizes micro-partitions in the background using compute credits to improve clustering depth for the specified key. This is an ongoing process, not an immediate one-time operation. A is wrong because reclustering is not free — it consumes compute credits. C is wrong because reclustering does reorganize existing data over time, not only new inserts. D is wrong because the CLUSTER BY statement does not lock the table.

questions.stem: Which cache type in Snowflake is stored on the SSD of virtual warehouse nodes and is lost when the warehouse is suspended?
questions.explanation: C is correct because the local disk cache (also called the data cache or warehouse cache) stores recently accessed micro-partition data on the SSD of warehouse nodes. It is ephemeral — when the warehouse suspends, the cache is lost. A is wrong because the query result cache is stored in the Cloud Services layer and persists for 24 hours regardless of warehouse state. B is wrong because the metadata cache is also in the Cloud Services layer and persists independently of warehouse state. D is wrong because 'remote storage cache' is not a Snowflake cache type.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator checks `SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION('orders', '(order_date)')` and receives an `average_depth` of 8. What does this indicate?

options.text: The table has 8 micro-partitions, which is optimal.
On average, 8 micro-partitions overlap for any given ORDER_DATE value, indicating poor clustering.
The clustering key spans 8 columns, which exceeds the recommended maximum.
8% of micro-partitions are fully pruned by the clustering key, which is suboptimal.

questions.explanation: B is correct because `average_depth` represents the average number of micro-partitions that overlap for any given value of the clustering key column(s). A lower depth means better clustering — a depth of 8 is high, meaning data for a given ORDER_DATE is spread across many micro-partitions and pruning will be less effective. A target depth is generally below 3 for actively queried tables. A is wrong because average_depth refers to overlap depth, not the count of micro-partitions. C is wrong because average_depth does not refer to the number of columns in the clustering key. D is wrong because average_depth is not a percentage metric.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature is MOST appropriate for dramatically improving the performance of point-lookup queries (equality filters on highly selective columns) on very large tables, without requiring manual partition management?
questions.explanation: B is correct because the Search Optimization Service builds a persistent search access path that dramatically improves the performance of equality and range predicate queries on selective high-cardinality columns — exactly the point-lookup use case. A is wrong because materialized views are beneficial for pre-aggregating complex queries but are not specifically designed for high-cardinality point lookups. C is wrong because clustering keys help with range scans and date-based filtering but are less effective for random equality lookups on high-cardinality columns. D is wrong because multi-cluster warehouses address concurrency, not individual query performance.

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to load data from files stored in an Amazon S3 bucket into a Snowflake table. Which Snowflake object is used to define the connection to the external S3 bucket?
questions.explanation: B is correct because an external stage is the Snowflake object that defines a reference to an external location (S3, Azure Blob, or GCS) including the URL and credentials (or storage integration). The COPY INTO command then uses the external stage to load data. A is wrong because an internal stage stores files within Snowflake's own managed storage, not external cloud storage. C is wrong because a Pipe (Snowpipe) is used for continuous data loading and references an existing stage — it does not define the external connection. D is wrong because a storage integration is a separate Snowflake object used for authentication to cloud storage, but it is not the stage itself.

questions.stem: A data engineer runs a COPY INTO command and sets ON_ERROR = 'SKIP_FILE'. A data file contains 5 rows and 2 of them have parse errors. What happens?

options.text: The 3 valid rows are loaded; the 2 error rows are skipped.
The entire file is skipped; no rows from that file are loaded.
The load aborts immediately and no files are processed.
The 2 error rows are logged and the remaining 3 rows are loaded after 5 minutes.

questions.explanation: B is correct because ON_ERROR = 'SKIP_FILE' causes Snowflake to skip the entire file if any error is encountered within it — none of the rows in that file are loaded. A is wrong because SKIP_FILE skips the entire file, not just the individual error rows. CONTINUE is the option that skips individual error rows and loads the rest. C is wrong because ABORT_STATEMENT is the option that aborts the entire COPY INTO operation immediately. D is wrong because there is no delayed retry mechanism in COPY INTO.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Loading — COPY INTO ON_ERROR Options

questions.stem: A team wants to continuously ingest JSON files from an Azure Blob Storage container into Snowflake with minimal latency. They need the ingestion to trigger automatically when new files arrive. Which configuration is MOST appropriate?

options.text: A Snowpipe with AUTO_INGEST = TRUE configured with Azure Event Grid notifications.
A scheduled task running COPY INTO every minute.
A Snowpipe called via the REST API on a fixed schedule.
An external stage with FORCE = TRUE to reload files continuously.

questions.explanation: A is correct because Snowpipe with AUTO_INGEST = TRUE on Azure uses Azure Event Grid notifications to automatically trigger file ingestion as soon as new files land in the Azure Blob container. This provides near-real-time, event-driven ingestion. B is wrong because a scheduled task with COPY INTO introduces unnecessary latency (the full interval between runs) and is not truly event-driven. C is wrong because the Snowpipe REST API requires external orchestration to call it, which defeats the purpose of automatic event-driven ingestion. D is wrong because FORCE = TRUE in COPY INTO simply forces reloading of already-loaded files and does not enable event-driven triggering.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Loading — Snowpipe with AUTO_INGEST

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the `METADATA$ACTION` column in a Snowflake stream?

options.text: It stores the user who made the change.
It indicates whether the row represents an INSERT or DELETE operation.
It contains a unique row identifier that never changes.
It stores the timestamp when the change was captured.

questions.explanation: B is correct because METADATA$ACTION contains either 'INSERT' or 'DELETE' to indicate the type of DML change captured in the stream. Updates appear as paired DELETE + INSERT rows. A is wrong because the stream does not capture the user who made the change; that information is in the query history. C is wrong because METADATA$ROW_ID is the column that provides a unique row identifier. D is wrong because the stream does not expose a dedicated timestamp column for the change time.

questions.stem: A data engineer creates a stream on a table that only receives INSERT operations (no UPDATEs or DELETEs). Which stream type provides the MOST efficient processing for this use case?
questions.explanation: B is correct because an append-only stream is optimized for tables that receive only insert operations. It tracks inserted rows without the overhead of tracking update/delete pairs, making it more efficient for insert-heavy workloads. A is wrong because a standard stream tracks all DML (inserts, updates, deletes), which adds overhead not needed for insert-only workloads. C is wrong because insert-only streams are specifically for external tables, not standard Snowflake tables. D is wrong because 'delta stream' is not a Snowflake stream type.

questions.stem: A task is created with the following DDL: `CREATE TASK load_task WAREHOUSE = 'TRANSFORM_WH' SCHEDULE = 'USING CRON 0 2 * * * America/New_York' AS INSERT INTO ...`. What must be done before this task will run?

options.text: Nothing; tasks start running immediately after creation.
The task must be resumed with ALTER TASK load_task RESUME.
The warehouse must be manually started before the scheduled time.
The task must be tested with EXECUTE TASK load_task before it can run on schedule.

questions.explanation: B is correct because tasks are created in a suspended state by default. They must be explicitly resumed using `ALTER TASK task_name RESUME` before they will execute on schedule. A is wrong because tasks do NOT start immediately — they begin in a suspended state. C is wrong because Snowflake automatically resumes the virtual warehouse when a task is scheduled to run (assuming the warehouse has auto-resume enabled). D is wrong because EXECUTE TASK is an optional manual trigger for testing; it is not required before scheduled runs.

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to implement a CDC pipeline using Snowflake streams and tasks. Which TWO statements about streams are CORRECT? (Select TWO)

options.text: A stream becomes stale if its offset advances beyond the Time Travel retention period of the source table.
Consuming a stream in a DML transaction advances the stream offset only if the transaction commits successfully.
Multiple streams on the same table share the same offset position.
SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA() returns TRUE even if no new rows have been inserted since the stream was last consumed.

questions.explanation: A and B are correct. A stream becomes stale if its offset falls behind beyond the Time Travel retention period — after that, the captured change data is no longer available. When a stream is consumed inside a DML transaction (e.g., INSERT INTO ... SELECT FROM stream), the offset advances only if the transaction commits; if it rolls back, the stream offset remains unchanged. C is wrong because each stream on a table maintains its own independent offset. D is wrong because SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA() returns FALSE when there is no new unconsumed data in the stream.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — Streams, Stale Streams

questions.stem: A developer creates a Dynamic Table with `TARGET_LAG = '5 minutes'`. Which statement BEST describes this setting?

options.text: Snowflake will refresh the dynamic table exactly every 5 minutes using a fixed cron schedule.
Snowflake guarantees the dynamic table data will never be more than 5 minutes behind its base tables.
Snowflake will attempt to refresh the dynamic table so that it lags its base tables by no more than 5 minutes, on a best-effort basis.
The dynamic table will only refresh when data older than 5 minutes is detected in the base tables.

questions.explanation: C is correct because TARGET_LAG specifies the target (not guaranteed) maximum staleness of the dynamic table relative to its base tables. Snowflake schedules refreshes to meet this target on a best-effort basis. A is wrong because TARGET_LAG is not a fixed cron schedule — it is a staleness target that drives Snowflake's automated refresh scheduling. B is wrong because the guarantee framing is too strong; it is a target, not a hard SLA. D is wrong because the refresh is not triggered by data age within the base tables beyond the lag window.

questions.stem: Which stored procedure execution mode allows the procedure to operate with the privileges of the role that calls the procedure, rather than the role that owns it?
questions.explanation: B is correct because EXECUTE AS CALLER causes the stored procedure to run with the privileges of the calling role, allowing the procedure's access to be scoped to what the caller can do. A is wrong because EXECUTE AS OWNER (the default) causes the procedure to run with the privileges of the procedure owner's role, regardless of who calls it. C is wrong because EXECUTE AS CURRENT_USER is not a valid Snowflake stored procedure execution option. D is wrong because EXECUTE AS SECURITYADMIN is not a valid option.
questions.reference: Snowflake Data Transformations — Stored Procedures Execution Rights

questions.stem: How long is Snowflake's Fail-Safe period for standard tables?
questions.explanation: B is correct because Snowflake Fail-Safe provides a 7-day period after the Time Travel retention period expires during which Snowflake can recover data for disaster recovery purposes. A is wrong because 1 day is the default Time Travel retention for Standard edition, not the Fail-Safe duration. C is wrong because 14 days is not the Fail-Safe duration. D is wrong because 30 days is not the Fail-Safe duration.

questions.stem: A data engineer accidentally drops a table in Snowflake. The table had a Time Travel retention period of 24 hours and was dropped 3 hours ago. Which command restores the table?
questions.explanation: B is correct because `UNDROP TABLE` restores a dropped table from Time Travel. Since the table was dropped only 3 hours ago and the retention period is 24 hours, the table is still within Time Travel and can be restored. A is wrong because RESTORE TABLE is not a valid Snowflake command. C is wrong because RECOVER TABLE is not a valid Snowflake command. D is wrong because AT(OFFSET => ...) queries historical data but does not restore a dropped table — it would fail since the table no longer exists.
